name: Fort Worth Independent School District
districtFact: Fort Worth ISD's attendance boundaries do not cover the entire city of Fort Worth — rapidly growing areas in north and west Fort Worth fall within neighboring districts including Keller ISD and Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD. A Fort Worth mailing address does not guarantee FWISD enrollment. For families targeting the Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts (K-12 fine arts magnet) or the Early College High School program at Tarrant County College, applications typically open in fall and close before the end of the calendar year. Arriving in January misses the window by three months.

about.title: About Fort Worth Independent School District
about.content:
Fort Worth Independent School District was founded in 1882 as one of Texas's original school systems, in what was then a frontier cattle town at the southern end of the Chisholm Trail. Today FWISD serves approximately 80,000 students across 150 schools — one of the 30 largest school districts in the United States — covering the City of Fort Worth and portions of Tarrant County.

Fort Worth's identity is distinct from Dallas in ways that shape the school system directly. The city's Western heritage is not background decoration: active cattle markets, the Fort Worth Stockyards, and the nation's oldest continuously operating weekly rodeo remain living institutions, and Agricultural Education and FFA programs are embedded throughout the FWISD curriculum. The Fort Worth Stock Show and Rodeo, running from late January through mid-February, is a standard field trip destination for agriculture, science, and social studies classes district-wide. Alongside this, Fort Worth maintains a genuine high-culture identity — Bass Performance Hall is a national-caliber venue with formal FWISD partnerships, and the Van Cliburn Foundation's relationship with the district produces subsidized student performances tied to the school calendar.

FWISD's student population is majority-minority: approximately two-thirds of students identify as Latino, and roughly 80% qualify for free or reduced-price lunch. The district's geographic footprint spans from the historic urban core to the rapidly developing north Tarrant County corridor surrounding Alliance Texas — one of the nation's largest inland ports — where corporate relocations drive consistent mid-year enrollment.

Naval Air Station Joint Reserve Base Fort Worth (NAS JRB, formerly Carswell AFB) is an active multi-branch reserve installation in Tarrant County. Military families at NAS JRB make up a meaningful share of FWISD's enrollment, with PCS transfers arriving throughout the year. The district has systems for expediting enrollment for military families with records in transit — bring immunization records, prior transcripts, and any IEP or 504 documentation when enrolling.

about.title: Signature Programs and School Choice
about.content:
FWISD operates several specialized programs that draw students from across the district — but each runs its own application process, and all have fall deadlines that pass before most relocating families arrive.

The Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts (FWAFA) is a K-12 fine arts magnet covering performing arts, visual arts, dance, and creative writing. FWAFA is the only school of its kind in Tarrant County and one of the few in Texas that runs K-12 in a single fine arts focus. Admissions include an audition or portfolio component. Applications open in October or November for the following year — families moving to Fort Worth after December are looking at a minimum 12-month wait before their child can enter.

Early College High School (ECHS) programs run in partnership with Tarrant County College (TCC). Students take college courses alongside their high school requirements and can graduate with up to 60 free college credit hours — two years of community college — already completed. This is not a gifted program; it's designed for motivated students who want to reduce the cost and time of a college degree. ECHS students follow a separate calendar that includes TCC orientation, registration windows, and semester schedules. Applications open in fall.

FWISD's Career and Technical Education (CTE) academies cover more than 60 industry pathways — aviation maintenance, healthcare, engineering, culinary, and advanced manufacturing among them. Several CTE tracks are aligned directly with hiring pipelines at Alliance Texas employers (Amazon fulfillment, American Airlines MRO, and defense contractors). Students finishing a full CTE sequence leave with industry certifications. Parents moving from districts where CTE is elective rather than credentialed should ask about certification requirements at enrollment.

Two campuses — Alice Carlson Applied Learning Center and Jo Kelly School — follow an Intersessional calendar with different break windows than the standard FWISD calendar. The ADSY program (Accelerated and Differentiated Support for Years) also has its own schedule. Families considering either should request the correct calendar before finalizing enrollment.

about.title: Before You Move to Fort Worth ISD
about.content:
Four steps that save significant time and frustration for families relocating to Fort Worth.

Verify your school district by exact address — not just city name. Fort Worth's rapid suburban growth has created a patchwork of school district boundaries across Tarrant County. Several areas within the City of Fort Worth city limits fall within Keller ISD, Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD, White Settlement ISD, or other neighboring districts. The FWISD school locator at fwisd.org uses your specific street address to confirm whether you are in FWISD territory and which campus serves your address. Two houses on the same street in a developing north Fort Worth area can fall in different districts if a boundary runs between them — verify before signing a lease or mortgage.

Check program application timelines before your move date. FWAFA and ECHS have fall application windows that close by November or December for the following school year. A family that arrives in Fort Worth in January cannot enroll in these programs until the next fall cycle. If a specific specialty program is a priority, time your move to arrive before November to allow a fall application.

Prepare for an early August start. FWISD typically begins the school year on or around August 10 — earlier than most school districts outside Texas. Summer childcare, camps, and travel plans that assume a late-August or post-Labor Day start will not work. Adjust childcare and summer programming before the move.

Build a tornado and severe weather plan. Tarrant County sits in Tornado Alley, and peak severe weather season — March through May — overlaps directly with the second half of the school year. FWISD uses shelter-in-place protocols during tornado warnings, and dismissal can be delayed on short notice when afternoon storm systems develop. Register for FWISD's emergency notifications at fwisd.org and establish a backup pickup plan before tornado season begins.

content: Fort Worth ISD ends the school year May 21 — about a week earlier than Dallas ISD and two weeks before Miami-Dade. Summer camps and childcare starting June 1 may leave a gap for Fort Worth families.

Fort Worth ISD and Dallas ISD are separate districts. If your family has children in both, confirm Spring Break and start dates on each district's calendar — they are usually close but not always identical.
Good Friday (March 26) is a school holiday in Fort Worth ISD. Families from states that don't observe Good Friday should mark this day off on their calendar — it falls one week after Spring Break.

ECHS students at Tarrant County College dual-enrollment schools may have additional orientation or testing days outside the standard calendar. Contact the campus directly for ECHS-specific scheduling.

STAAR testing for grades 3–8 runs in April and May — the last six weeks of school are the least flexible for scheduling vacations, appointments, or family travel. If you need to pull a child from school during May, plan it for the first two weeks rather than the final stretch.

Spring Break (typically mid-March) and Good Friday (one week later) together create two disrupted weeks in quick succession. Childcare for both needs to be booked by January — most Fort Worth providers fill Spring Break slots by February. If you're moving from a state that doesn't observe Good Friday, add it to your calendar now.

Families moving from Dallas ISD should not assume identical schedules. Spring Break usually aligns, but FWISD ends roughly a week earlier — which affects summer camp start dates, childcare transitions, and any plans tied to a 'Memorial Day school's-out' assumption that works in Dallas but not here.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fort Worth ISD the same as Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fort Worth ISD and Dallas ISD are separate, independent school districts. Fort Worth ISD serves the city of Fort Worth and parts of Tarrant County; Dallas ISD serves the city of Dallas and parts of Dallas County. Despite sharing DFW Airport and the same metropolitan area, they have different school boards, different calendars, and sometimes different school start and end dates. Spring Break typically aligns between the two districts, but instructional day counts and end-of-year dates have differed historically. Always check each district's official calendar separately.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fort Worth ISD serve all addresses in the city of Fort Worth?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fort Worth ISD boundaries do not cover the entire city of Fort Worth. Several areas within Fort Worth city limits — particularly in north and west Fort Worth — fall within neighboring districts including Keller ISD, Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD, and White Settlement ISD. A Fort Worth mailing address does not guarantee FWISD enrollment. Use the school locator at fwisd.org with your exact street address to confirm which district and campus serve your home before signing a lease or purchase agreement.

districtFaqs.q: Why does Fort Worth ISD sometimes end the school year earlier than Dallas ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD's instructional day count has ranged from 173 to 175 days in recent years — fewer than the Texas state minimum of 180 instructional days. FWISD qualifies for TEA waivers that substitute extended instructional minutes (longer class periods) for calendar days, allowing the district to meet state requirements with a shorter school year. Dallas ISD typically runs closer to 180 calendar instructional days. The result is that FWISD ends approximately one week earlier than Dallas ISD in most years. Check the current school year's official FWISD calendar for the confirmed instructional day count and last day, as both can vary year to year.

districtFaqs.q: Does Fort Worth ISD observe Good Friday as a school holiday?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD consistently observes Good Friday as a school holiday. Good Friday falls on a different date each year — check the current FWISD academic calendar for the confirmed date. In years where Good Friday falls one week after Spring Break, FWISD families experience two consecutive short school weeks in late March. Good Friday observance is common in Texas school districts but is not observed in most other states — families relocating from outside Texas should add the date to their childcare calendar.

districtFaqs.q: How does Fort Worth ISD compare with Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD or Keller ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD, Eagle Mountain-Saginaw ISD (EMSISD), and Keller ISD are separate, independent school districts in Tarrant County. FWISD is the largest by enrollment, serving the urban core. EMSISD and Keller ISD are suburban districts with their own school boards and academic calendars — spring break dates and first/last days may differ from FWISD by a few days to a week. Families purchasing homes in north Fort Worth suburbs should confirm which specific district serves their address before finalizing enrollment, as the boundary between FWISD and its suburban neighbors runs through developing neighborhoods where the lines are not obvious.

districtFaqs.q: What is the Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts and how do I apply?
districtFaqs.a: The Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts (FWAFA) is a selective K-12 fine arts magnet school operated by FWISD, offering programs in performing arts, visual arts, dance, and creative writing. Enrollment is open to students district-wide regardless of home address; admissions include an audition or portfolio review component. FWAFA is one of the few dedicated fine arts schools of its scale in North Texas, and applications are competitive. Applications typically open in October or November for the following school year — families interested in FWAFA enrollment should contact the school at fwisd.org well before November. Families arriving in Fort Worth after December miss the application window and must wait until the next fall cycle.

districtFaqs.q: What is an Early College High School in Fort Worth ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD operates Early College High School (ECHS) programs in partnership with Tarrant County College (TCC). ECHS students complete dual-enrollment college coursework alongside their high school requirements, earning up to 60 college credit hours — a full associate degree — at no cost by graduation. ECHS students follow a modified calendar that may include additional TCC orientation days, registration periods, and campus schedules not reflected in the standard FWISD academic calendar. Applications for ECHS programs typically open in fall — contact the specific ECHS campus directly for current application timelines and eligibility requirements.

districtFaqs.q: How does Fort Worth ISD handle tornado or severe weather closures?
districtFaqs.a: Fort Worth ISD follows shelter-in-place protocols during tornado warnings — students remain at school in interior rooms until the warning is lifted, which can delay dismissal by minutes to hours depending on storm duration. Tornado season in Tarrant County peaks from March through May, overlapping the second half of the school year. FWISD communicates dismissal delays and schedule changes through its parent notification system; register before March so you receive alerts when they matter. Families should have a backup pickup plan for weather-delay days, as late afternoon storm systems in North Texas can develop with little advance notice.

districtFaqs.q: When does kindergarten registration open for Fort Worth ISD?
districtFaqs.a: FWISD kindergarten registration typically opens in February or March for the following fall school year. Texas requires children to be five years old by September 1 to enter kindergarten. Required documents typically include: certified birth certificate, current Texas immunization record, and two proofs of FWISD residency (utility bill, lease, or mortgage statement). Families relocating to Fort Worth in spring or summer should contact their assigned neighborhood school directly rather than waiting for a district-wide registration event. Use the school locator at fwisd.org to confirm your assigned school before registration.

districtFaqs.q: How do I enroll a child mid-year in Fort Worth ISD?
districtFaqs.a: FWISD accepts mid-year enrollment at the neighborhood school assigned to your address throughout the school year. Required documents: proof of age (birth certificate or passport), current Texas immunization records, proof of FWISD residency, and academic records from the prior school. FWISD will accept unofficial transcripts or teacher-generated records on a provisional basis while official records are in transit — useful for families relocating quickly for military PCS orders or corporate transfers. Contact your assigned campus at fwisd.org to schedule an enrollment appointment; processing typically takes one to three business days.

livingHere.intro: Fort Worth has a distinct identity from Dallas — rooted in cowboy heritage, Western culture, and a strong arts scene — despite sharing the DFW airport and metropolitan footprint. For families new to Tarrant County, a few local realities shape how the school calendar interacts with life in the city.

highlights.label: Fort Worth Stock Show, Rodeo, and School Life
highlights.detail: The Fort Worth Stock Show and Rodeo (typically late January through mid-February) is one of Texas's largest annual events and a regular FWISD field trip destination — agriculture, FFA (Future Farmers of America), and science classes attend during the show's three-week run. The Stockyards National Historic District sees peak tourism during this period and again at Spring Break; families planning local outings should expect higher crowds. On school days during Rodeo season, families driving near the Stockyards area should allow extra commute time on North Main Street and I-35W.

highlights.label: DFW Airport Proximity
highlights.detail: DFW International Airport sits between Fort Worth and Dallas, roughly 20 miles from most Fort Worth ISD schools. Spring Break departures from DFW fill quickly — Fort Worth ISD's March 15–19 break aligns with Dallas ISD, creating simultaneous peak demand at the airport.

highlights.label: NAS JRB Fort Worth and Military Family Enrollment
highlights.detail: Naval Air Station Joint Reserve Base Fort Worth (formerly Carswell AFB) is an active multi-branch reserve installation within Tarrant County. Military families stationed at NAS JRB are part of the Fort Worth ISD enrollment base, with PCS transfers arriving throughout the school year. Bring immunization records, prior school transcripts, and any IEP or 504 documentation when enrolling — FWISD can typically complete placement within a few business days. Military families departing on PCS orders should request a student transfer record packet from their campus before the move to speed enrollment at the receiving district.

highlights.label: Tornado Season
highlights.detail: Tarrant County sits in Tornado Alley, with peak severe weather risk March through May. Fort Worth ISD uses shelter-in-place protocols; late-afternoon storm systems can delay dismissal on short notice. Families should have a backup pickup plan for weather-delay days.

highlights.label: Bass Performance Hall and FWISD Arts Programs
highlights.detail: Bass Performance Hall in downtown Fort Worth is a major FWISD educational partner — the district has formal partnerships with the Fort Worth Symphony Orchestra and the Van Cliburn Foundation, resulting in subsidized student performances and curriculum-tied field trips throughout the school year. Elementary through high school students attend Bass Hall on school days as part of arts integration programs; permission slips for these trips are a regular occurrence for FWISD families. Students at the Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts also use the Sundance Square corridor for performances tied to the school calendar.

highlights.label: Alliance Texas and North Corridor Growth
highlights.detail: Alliance Texas — one of the nation's largest inland ports — brings thousands of jobs to north Tarrant County in aviation, logistics, e-commerce, and manufacturing. Corporate relocations and hires for Alliance-area employers (including Amazon fulfillment, American Airlines Maintenance, and industrial manufacturers) drive consistent mid-year enrollment into north Fort Worth ISD schools. FWISD has opened new schools in the north corridor to keep pace with growth, and attendance boundaries in this area have been adjusted in recent years — families settling in north Fort Worth should verify their school assignment at fwisd.org using their specific address.

highlights.label: FWISD Signature Programs
highlights.detail: Fort Worth ISD operates distinctive programs not available in most neighboring districts. The Fort Worth Academy of Fine Arts (FWAFA) is a selective K-12 fine arts magnet serving the performing arts, visual arts, and creative writing — one of the few dedicated fine arts schools of its scale in North Texas. The district's Career and Technical Education (CTE) academies span 60+ industry pathways including aviation technology, healthcare, engineering, and culinary arts, with students earning industry certifications before graduation. Early College High School (ECHS) programs in partnership with Tarrant County College allow students to graduate with up to 60 free college credit hours. Applications for FWAFA and ECHS programs typically open in fall — families interested in specialty programs should contact FWISD's school choice office by October.

highlights.label: FWISD Annual Planning Rhythm
highlights.detail: Fort Worth ISD follows a distinct annual cycle that families benefit from mapping at the start of each school year. August 10: school begins — one of Texas's earliest start dates, with UIL athletic season launching simultaneously. January–February: Fort Worth Stock Show and Rodeo, with FWISD field trips running throughout the three-week event. March 15–19: Spring Break aligned with Dallas ISD. March 26: Good Friday school holiday, creating a long weekend one week after return from break. May 21: last day — a week earlier than Dallas ISD and two weeks before most national peers, making summer camp and childcare planning especially important. ECHS and CTE students follow an additional layer: fall Tarrant County College orientation, spring industry certification testing, and internship cycles that run parallel to the standard school calendar.

compareIntro: Fort Worth ISD and Dallas ISD share DFW Airport and the same metropolitan area, but they are separate districts with separate school boards — and their calendars are not identical. Spring Break typically aligns between the two (both usually break the same week), but FWISD's lower instructional day count means it ends one to two weeks earlier than Dallas ISD in most years. Families with children in both districts, or comparing before a move, should check both official calendars rather than assuming DFW = one shared schedule.

relatedDistricts.name: Dallas Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Dallas ISD and Fort Worth ISD are the two anchor districts of the DFW metro, separated by about 30 miles and served by the same airport. Spring Break usually aligns — both districts typically break the same week in March, creating simultaneous peak demand at DFW for departing families. Where they diverge is instructional days: Dallas ISD runs closer to the Texas state minimum of 180 instructional days, while FWISD has used TEA waivers to operate on 173–175 days, resulting in FWISD ending roughly one week earlier each year. Dallas ISD is also significantly larger (around 140,000 students vs FWISD's 80,000) and serves a more geographically dispersed urban area. Families choosing between the two cities for a relocation should verify the current year's end dates — the gap changes slightly year to year.

relatedDistricts.name: Houston Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Houston ISD and Fort Worth ISD are both large Texas urban districts, but they operate under very different conditions. HISD serves nearly 194,000 students in Harris County — more than twice FWISD's enrollment — and its calendar is shaped by Atlantic hurricane season (June–November) in ways that Tarrant County never experiences. HISD also has a larger magnet school network and has been under TEA state conservatorship since 2023. The two districts' Spring Break windows frequently fall in different weeks: HISD typically breaks in early March while FWISD breaks in mid-March. Families relocating between Houston and Fort Worth — common moves for energy sector and aerospace employees — should not assume calendar alignment and should verify both districts' current-year schedules.
